title: Wood Cooler Stand with Lid and Shelf
summary: A slatted wood housing that dresses up a plastic cooler, with a mitered hinged lid and a drop-in shelf below.
description:
This build wraps a standard plastic cooler in a real furniture frame — a slatted-front box with a mitered hinged lid on top and an arched-bracket shelf underneath for cups or supplies. Because it's built around one specific cooler's footprint, measure your own cooler before cutting a single board; the plan's dimensions are a starting point, not a guarantee, and coolers vary in size between brands.

The lid is the fussiest part: its frame is mitered and beveled at 45° so it closes flush over the box opening, and the inner frame glued underneath is what actually registers the lid's position and keeps it from sliding off-center. Get the inner frame sized and placed before you worry about hinges — a well-fit inner frame makes the hinges nearly foolproof, while a sloppy one fights you the whole way.

tools.note: For the 45° mitered and beveled lid frame.

materials.name: Plastic cooler
materials.note: Measure yours before cutting — sizes vary by brand, and this plan is built around one specific footprint.

cutList.note: Both ends mitered 45°, not parallel, long-point measurement.
Both ends mitered 45°, not parallel, long-point measurement.
Both ends beveled 45°, not parallel, long-point measurement.
Both ends beveled 45°, not parallel, long-point measurement.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: The cut list names every part simply Pine, so sort by stock first. From the 2x2 cut four legs at 35", six front/back frame rails at 24" and two front panel, lower at 10-3/4". From the 1x3 cut five back panel boards at 10-3/4", two side panel, lower at 8-3/4", two top frame ends at 28", two top frame sides at 17-1/2", two lid frame ends at 27", two lid frame sides at 16" and one lid top panel edge at 14-1/2". From the 1x6 cut four front panel boards at 10-3/4", four side panel boards at 8-3/4", four lid top panel boards at 14-1/2", two shelf supports at 5-1/2" and one shelf at 15". From the 2x4 cut the 14 side/shelf slats at 13-1/2". From the 1x2 cut two lid inner frame ends at 13" and two lid inner frame sides at 25-1/2". Cut the top-frame and lid-frame pieces last and at 45°, mitred for the top frame and bevelled for the lid, with every stated length taken long point to long point. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut that comes to four 8 ft 2x2, three 8 ft 1x3, two 8 ft 1x6, two 8 ft 2x4 and one 8 ft 1x2 — exactly what the materials list declares, with no spare on any of them. Measure your own cooler before you cut a single board and adjust these figures if it differs: the whole box is built around one specific footprint.

steps.title: Build the two main frames
steps.body: Each of the two main frames is two 35" 2x2 legs with three 24" frame rails between them — one at the top flush with the leg tops, one near the bottom, and one at the height you want the lower shelf. With the rails fitting between the legs, each frame comes out 27" wide overall and 35" tall, and both must match to the 1/16": mark all four legs together, clamped as a stack, so the rail positions cannot drift between the front and back frames. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes in the rail ends and glue every joint. Be aware that the only pocket screw in the materials list is 1-1/4", which is short for joining 1-1/2" stock — 2-1/2" pocket screws are what these joints really want, so add them or back the joints up with countersunk screws. Check both diagonals on each frame before the glue grabs.

steps.title: Panel the back frame
steps.body: Fasten the five 10-3/4" 1x3 back panel boards to the back frame, running them vertically with their faces on the outside of the frame. Five boards at 2-1/2" wide cover 12-1/2" of the frame's 27" width, so the back is meant to read as spaced slats rather than a solid panel — lay all five out across the frame and set the gaps even by eye before you fasten anything, and expect the gap to shift if your actual board widths run under nominal. Keep the top ends aligned against a straightedge and screw through each board into the rails behind it. This is the face that will end up against a wall, so it is also the forgiving one to practise your spacing on.

steps.title: Panel the front frame
steps.body: Fasten the four 10-3/4" 1x6 front panel boards to the front frame the same way, vertical and on the outside face. Four boards at 5-1/2" give 22" of coverage and the two 10-3/4" 2x2 lower front pieces add another 3", so the front and back are deliberately different — the front reads nearly solid against the back's open slats. Before you fasten the outermost boards, drill 3/4" pocket holes on their edges facing the 2x2 legs: the lower front pieces have to be attached in a corner you will not be able to reach once the panelling is on, and this is the moment that costs you nothing. Screw through into the rails, keeping the tops aligned.

steps.title: Add the lower front piece
steps.body: Fit the two 10-3/4" 2x2 lower front pieces using the pocket holes you drilled in the previous step, or countersunk screws driven from inside while you can still get a driver in. Decide now how they sit relative to the 1x6 boards beside them: a 2x2 is 1-1/2" thick against the 1x6's 3/4", so they either stand proud as a deliberate detail or get set back flush, and whichever you choose both sides have to match or the front reads crooked. Glue as well as screw — a front panel that works loose will rattle every time the lid drops.

steps.title: Connect the frames and add slats
steps.body: Stand the front and back frames parallel and join them with the 13-1/2" 2x4 side/shelf slats, which are what set the stand's depth: 13-1/2" between the frames plus a 1-1/2" leg at each side makes the housing 16-1/2" deep against 27" wide. All fourteen slats do double duty — some close in the sides, the rest form the lower shelf platform — and the source spaces them roughly 1-1/4" apart, so cut a 1-1/4" offcut and use it as a spacer instead of eyeballing each gap. Screw through the frame legs and rails into the ends of every slat, and check the assembly for square as you go; once more than a few slats are in, the box permanently holds whatever shape you gave it. Keep the run of slats consistent from side to side, because the side panel boards in the next step have to land on them.

steps.title: Fill in the side panels
steps.body: Close in each side with two 8-3/4" 1x6 side panel boards and one 8-3/4" 1x3 side panel lower piece. Their widths are the check that the frame went together right: 5-1/2" plus 5-1/2" plus 2-1/2" is exactly 13-1/2", which is the clear span between the front and back frames, so on each side those three boards should fill the opening edge to edge with no gap at all. If they do not, your frames are not the 13-1/2" apart the slats set — stop and fix that before you fasten, because everything above depends on the housing being 27" x 16-1/2". Screw them to the legs and into the slats behind them.

steps.title: Build and attach the top frame
steps.body: Build the top frame flat on the bench from the two 28" ends and two 17-1/2" sides, all 1x3 mitred at 45° at both ends with those lengths taken long point to long point. Clamp the mitres tight, drill 3/4" pocket holes on the underside and pull each corner together with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue — putting the pocket holes underneath keeps the top face clean. The finished frame is 28" x 17-1/2" outside, which gives a 1/2" overhang all round over the 27" x 16-1/2" housing, and subtracting the 2-1/2" stock from each side leaves a 23" x 12-1/2" opening in the middle. Set it on, check the overhang is even on all four sides, and fasten it down with countersunk screws or nails. Then test-fit the cooler: it should drop through that opening and sit level before you spend any time on the lid.

steps.title: Build the lid
steps.body: Build the lid frame the same way from the two 27" ends and two 16" sides, 1x3 bevelled at 45° at both ends, long point to long point, so it closes as a 27" x 16" rectangle — smaller than the 28" x 17-1/2" top frame beneath it, sitting 1/2" in at each end and 3/4" in at each side. Then lay the four 14-1/2" 1x6 lid top panel boards and the single 14-1/2" 1x3 edge piece across the frame. Be warned that they do not close: four boards at 5-1/2" plus one at 2-1/2" is 24-1/2" of panel against the frame's 27" length, so the panel will fall 2-1/2" short and the source does not say where that shortfall goes. Decide before you glue anything — rip the edge piece wider from an offcut, or run an even reveal at both ends — rather than discovering it with the boards already down. Fasten the panel to the frame with glue and screws driven from underneath so the lid's top face shows no fasteners.

steps.title: Add the lid's inner registration frame and hang it
steps.body: The inner frame glued to the underside of the lid is what registers the lid over the box opening and stops it sliding off-centre, so it has to be right before you touch a hinge. Be warned that the cut list does not close here: the top frame's opening is 23" x 12-1/2", but the lid inner frame's parts are 25-1/2" and 13", so its smallest possible outside size is 25-1/2" x 13" — too big to drop into that opening and too small to register around the outside of the 28" x 17-1/2" top frame. As drawn it fits neither way. Measure your own top frame opening instead, cut the inner frame to drop into it with about 1/8" of clearance all round, and treat the 25-1/2" and 13" figures as wrong rather than trying to force them. Once it drops in cleanly with the lid sitting centred, glue the inner frame to the lid's underside, let it cure fully, and only then hang the lid on its hinges along the back edge.

steps.title: Build and mount the shelf
steps.body: Shape the two 5-1/2" x 5-1/2" shelf supports into brackets: mark a curve on one, cut it, then use it as a template for the second so the pair matches exactly — or, if you would rather not saw a curve with the tools this plan lists, simply mitre a 45° corner off each block, which reads just as intentional. Sand the cut edge before it goes on, because you will not reach it afterwards. Fasten one bracket to each 2x2 leg at the shelf height with pocket screws or countersunk screws, both measured from the floor so the shelf cannot sit crooked, then set the 15" x 5-1/2" shelf on top of them and screw it down through into the brackets. Note that both the shelf and its brackets come from the 1x6, not the 1x2 — the 1x2 in this build is the lid's inner frame.

steps.title: Add casters (optional)
steps.body: If you'd like the stand to roll, install caster wheels at the base of each leg.

images.alt: Wood cooler stand with a slatted front, hinged mitered lid, and lower shelf
